concepts.title: Spring Mass System (Example of SHM)
concepts.conceptBody:
Spring Mass System (Example of SHM)

If the spring is un stretched, there is no net force on the mass - in other words, the system is in equilibrium. However, if the mass is displaced from equilibrium, the spring will exert a restoring force, which is a force that tends to restore it to the equilibrium position. In the case of the spring-mass system, this force is the elastic force, which is given by Hooke's Law, $\text{F}=-\text{kx}$, where $\text{F}$ is the restoring force, $\text{x}$ is the displacement, and $\text{k}$ is the spring constant.

Any system that undergoes simple harmonic motion exhibits two key features.

  1. When the system is displaced from equilibrium there must exist a restoring force that tends to restore it to equilibrium.
  2. The restoring force must be proportional to displacement, or approximately so.

The spring-mass system satisfies both

Once the mass is displaced it experiences a restoring force, accelerating it, causing it to start going back to the equilibrium position. As it gets closer to equilibrium the restoring force decreases; at the equilibrium position the restoring force is 0 . However, at $\text{x}=0$, the mass has some momentum due to the impulse of the force that has acted on it; this causes the mass to shoot past the equilibrium position, in the case, compressing the spring. The restoring force then tends to slow it down, until the velocity reaches 0 , whereby it will attempt to reach equilibrium position again.

As long as the system does not lose energy, the mass will continue to oscillate like so; thus, the motion is termed periodic motion. Further analysis will show that in the case of the spring-mass system the motion is simple harmonic.

concepts.title: Spring Pendulum
concepts.conceptBody:
Spring Pendulum 

A point mass suspended from a massless spring constitutes a linear harmonic spring pendulum. If $\text{y}$ is a small extension or compression in the spring from the equilibrium state, the restoring force produced is given by

$\text{F}=-\text{ky},\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\text{ m}\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}\text{y}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}=-\text{ky }\left[ \text{asF}=\text{ma}=\text{m}\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}\text{y}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}} \right]$

Or $\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}\text{y}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}=-\frac{\text{k}}{\text{m}}\text{y }$ i.e., $\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}\text{y}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}=-{{\omega }^{2}}\text{y }$ with ${{\omega }^{2}}=\frac{\text{k}}{\text{m}}$

This is the standard equation of linear simple harmonic motion with time period $\text{T}=\left( 2\pi /\omega \right)$. So here

$\text{T}=2\pi \sqrt{\left( \frac{\text{m}}{\text{k}} \right)}\text{ or }\text{ f}=\frac{1}{2\pi }\sqrt{\left( \frac{\text{k}}{\text{m}} \right)}$

This is the required result and from this it is clear that:

(1) Time period of a spring pendulum is independent of acceleration due to gravity. This is why a clock based on spring pendulum will keep proper time everywhere on a hill or moon or in a satellite and time period of a spring pendulum will not change inside a liquid if damping effects are neglected.

(2) Time period of a spring pendulum depends on the mass suspended, i.e.,

$\text{T}\propto \sqrt{\text{m}}\text{ or }\text{ f}\propto \left( 1/\sqrt{\text{m}} \right)$

i.e., greater the mass greater will be the inertia and so lesser will be the frequency of oscillations and greater will be the time period.

(3) The time period depends on the force constant $\text{k}$ of the spring, i.e.,

$\text{T}\propto \left( 1/\sqrt{\text{k}} \right)\text{ or }\text{ f}\propto \sqrt{\text{k}}$

i.e., greater the force constant greater will be the frequency and lesser will be the time period. [The force constant $\text{k}$ of a spring is inversely proportional to its natural length.]

(4) If the spring has a mass $M$ and mass $m$ is suspended from it, effective mass is given by

${{\text{m}}_{\text{eff }}}=\text{m}+\left( \text{M}/3 \right)\text{ so }\text{ that }\text{ T}=2\pi \sqrt{\left[ {{\text{m}}_{\text{eff }}} \right]/\text{k}}$

(5) If two masses of mass ${{\text{m}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$ are connected by a spring and made to oscillate on horizontal surface, the reduced mass $m$ is given by

$\frac{1}{\text{ m}}=\frac{1}{{{\text{m}}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{m}}_{2}}}\text{ so }\text{ that }\text{ T}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\text{m}}{\text{k}}}$

(6) If a spring pendulum, oscillating in a vertical plane is made to oscillate on a horizontal surface, (or an inclined plane) time period will remain unchanged. However, equilibrium position for a spring in a horizontal plane is the position of natural length of spring as weight is balanced by reaction [Fig. (B)]. While in case of vertical motion equilibrium position will be $\text{L}+{{\text{y}}_{0}}$ with $\text{k}{{\text{y}}_{0}}=\text{mg}$ (Fig.)

(7) If the stretch in a vertically loaded spring is ${{\text{y}}_{0}}$ then for equilibrium of mass $\text{m}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{k}{{\text{y}}_{0}}=\text{mg}, \text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\left( \text{m}/\text{k} \right)=\left( {{\text{y}}_{0}}/\text{g} \right) \\ \text{T}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\text{m}}{\text{k}}}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{{{\text{y}}_{0}}}{\text{ g}}} \end{array}$

Here is must be kept in mind that time period is independent of ' $\text{g}$ ' as with change in $\text{g},{{\text{y}}_{0}}$ will also change in such a way that ${{\text{y}}_{0}}/\text{g}[\text{m}/\text{m}$ ) remains constant.

(8) Composite-spring pendulum : If a spring pendulum is constructed by using two springs and a mass, the following three situations are possible :

for these cases we have respectively

(A)

$\begin{matrix} \frac{1}{\text{k}}=\frac{1}{{{\text{k}}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{k}}_{2}}} \\ \text{ So }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\text{m}\left( {{\text{k}}_{1}}+{{\text{k}}_{2}} \right)}{{{\text{k}}_{1}}{{\text{k}}_{2}}}} \\ \end{matrix}$

(B)

$\text{k}={{\text{k}}_{1}}+{{\text{k}}_{2}}$

$\text{T}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\text{m}}{\left( {{\text{k}}_{1}}+{{\text{k}}_{2}} \right)}}$ 
(C)

$\text{k}={{\text{k}}_{1}}+{{\text{k}}_{2}}$

$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\left(\mathrm{k}_1+\mathrm{k}_2\right)}}$

concepts.title: Oscillation of Fluid Column
concepts.conceptBody:
Oscillations of a Fluid Column 

Initially the level of liquid in both the columns is same. The area of cross-section of the tube is uniform. If the liquid is depressed by $\text{x}$ in one limb, it will rise by $\text{x}$ along the length of the tube is the other limb. Here, the restoring force is provided by the hydrostatic pressure difference.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{F} & ~=-\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ P} \right)\text{A}=-\left( {{\text{h}}_{1}}+{{\text{h}}_{2}} \right)\rho \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g} \\ {} & ~=-\rho \text{gA}\left( \text{sin}{{\theta }_{1}}+\text{sin}{{\theta }_{2}} \right)\text{x} \\ \end{array}$

Let, $\text{m}$ be the mass of the liquid in the tube. Then,

$\text{ma}=-\rho \text{gA}\left( \text{sin}{{\theta }_{1}}+\text{sin}{{\theta }_{2}} \right)\text{x}$

Since, $\text{F}$ or a is proportional to $-\text{x}$, the motion of the liquid column in simple harmonic in nature, time period of which is given by,

$\text{T}=2\pi \sqrt{\left| \frac{\text{x}}{\text{a}} \right|}$

or

$\text{T}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\text{m}}{\rho \text{gA}\left( \text{sin}{{\theta }_{1}}+\text{sin}{{\theta }_{2}} \right)}}$

Note : For a U-tube if the liquid is filled to the height $1,{{\theta }_{1}}={{90}^{0}}={{\theta }_{2}}$ and $\text{m}=2$ (lA $\rho $ )

So,

$\text{T}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\text{l}}{\text{g}}}$

Thus, we see that the expression $\text{T}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}}}$ comes in pictures at three places

(i) Time period of a simple pendulum for small oscillations.

(ii) Time period of a spring-block system in vertical position.

(iii) Time period of a liquid column in U-tube filled to a height 1.

But l has different meanings at different places.

concepts.title: Composition of Two SHM
concepts.conceptBody:
Composition of Two SHMs 

If the particle is acted upon by two separate forces each of which can produce a simple harmonic motion, the resultant motion of the particle is a combination of two simple harmonic motions.

Let $\overrightarrow{{{r}_{1}}}$ denote the position of the particle at time $t$ of the force $\overrightarrow{{{F}_{1}}}$ alone acts on it. Similarly, at $\overrightarrow{{{r}_{2}}}$ denote the position at the time ' $\text{t}$ ' if the force $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{2}}}$ alone acts on it. According to Newton's second law of motion,

$\frac{\text{m}{{\text{d}}^{2}}{{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}={{\vec{F}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{m}{{\text{d}}^{2}}{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}={{\vec{F}}_{2}}$

Adding them,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{m}\left( \frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}{{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}+\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}={{{\vec{F}}}_{1}}+{{{\vec{F}}}_{2}} \right) \\ {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}\left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}+{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}} \right)={{{\vec{F}}}_{1}}+{{{\vec{F}}}_{2}} \\ \end{array}$

But ${{\vec{F}}_{1}}+{{\vec{F}}_{2}}$ is the resultant force acting on the particle and so the position of $\vec{r}$ of the particle when both the forces acted act is given by

$\text{m}\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}\vec{r}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}={{\vec{F}}_{1}}+{{\vec{F}}_{2}}$

Comparing equations (i) & (ii) we can show that

\(\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}_2}\) and \(=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}_2}\)

If these conditions are met at $\text{t}=0$, the actual position of the particle is given by the vector sum of $\overrightarrow{{{r}_{1}}}\And \overrightarrow{{{r}_{2}}}$.

concepts.title: Superposition of SHM
concepts.conceptBody:
Superposition of SHM

Let the direction be x-axis and the simple harmonic motions produced by two forces ${{\vec{F}}_{1}}\And {{\vec{F}}_{2}}$ be ${{\text{x}}_{1}}={{\text{A}}_{1}}\text{sin}\omega \text{t}$

${{\text{x}}_{2}}={{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)$ respectively

From above discussion, the resultant position of the particle is then,

$\text{x}={{\text{x}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{2}}={{\text{A}}_{1}}\text{sin}\omega \text{t}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)$

Put, ${{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi =\text{Acos}\delta $

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\phi =\text{Asin}\delta \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A} & ~=\sqrt{{{\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\phi \right)}^{2}}} \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A} & ~=\sqrt{\text{A}_{1}^{2}+\text{A}_{2}^{2}+2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi } \\ \end{array}$

and $\text{x}=\text{Acos}\delta \text{sin}\omega \text{t}+\text{Asin}\delta \text{cos}\omega \text{t}$

$\text{x}=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\delta \right)$

And $\text{tan}\delta =\frac{{{\text{A}}_{2}}+\text{sin}\phi }{{{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi }$

The amplitude of resultant simple harmonic motion is given by

$\text{A}=\sqrt{\text{A}_{1}^{2}+\text{A}_{2}^{2}+2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi }$

It is maximum when $\phi =0$

${{A}_{\text{max }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\sqrt{{{\left( {{A}_{1}}+{{A}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}}={{A}_{1}}+{{A}_{2}}$

It is minimum when $\text{cos}\phi =-1$ i.e. $\phi =\pi $

${{A}_{\text{min }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\sqrt{{{\left( {{A}_{1}}+{{A}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}}={{A}_{1}}-{{A}_{2}}$

Thus, we can see that this is similar to the vector addition. The same method of vector addition can be applied to the combination of more than two simple harmonic motions.
